_meta.description: 7 viral psychological triggers for JoyMaze content. Every piece of content targets exactly ONE primary trigger. Trigger determines the image composition, caption arc, and hook selection.
_meta.research_basis: Journal of Consumer Psychology 2024: 72% of shares are emotion-driven. Pinterest: >2% 24hr save rate = viral. Multiple emotional peaks outperform single peak. Saves today = traffic in 2-3 months.
_meta.rule: A trigger is only valid if a parent would FEEL it from the image ALONE — without reading any text. If the trigger requires the caption to work, the image has failed.

IDENTITY_MIRROR.label: Identity Mirror
IDENTITY_MIRROR.description: Parent shares because it reflects who they ARE or aspire to be. The share is a public identity statement: 'I am the kind of parent who chooses this.'
IDENTITY_MIRROR.psychology: Social identity theory — we broadcast content that confirms our self-concept. Sharing = identity assertion. For parents: 'I am intentional, screen-conscious, engaged.'
IDENTITY_MIRROR.activation_condition: Must show a parent CHOICE, not just an activity. The deliberate, quiet decision to do something better.
IDENTITY_MIRROR.image_rule: Parent visible in scene, at the edge of frame — watching from doorway or sideline. Quiet pride, not performed joy. The choice is already made. No uncertainty in the body language.
caption_opener_examples: This is what it looks like when a parent says no to the easy thing.
Some parents Google 'screen time guilt' at 11pm. She stopped searching.
The kind of afternoon she'll never remember. The kind she'll always remember feeling.

SCREEN_RELIEF.label: Screen-Time Relief
SCREEN_RELIEF.description: Opens the #1 parenting anxiety (screen-time guilt), then delivers exact relief. The emotional distance between tension and release IS the share trigger.
SCREEN_RELIEF.psychology: Loss aversion + relief activation. Parents carry pre-loaded screen-time guilt from cultural messaging. Content that names that anxiety and resolves it with a specific, real moment = save reflex.
SCREEN_RELIEF.activation_condition: Must first ACKNOWLEDGE the anxiety (without preaching), then RESOLVE it with a concrete, specific moment. The relief must feel earned, not told.
SCREEN_RELIEF.image_rule: Tablet face-down, TV remote pushed aside — the abandoned alternative MUST be visible. Child absorbed in printed activity. The discarded screen IS the message. No text needed.
caption_opener_examples: Forty-five minutes passed. She never looked up. The guilt didn't come.
The screen is right there. She hasn't touched it.
I waited for her to get bored. She never did.

CURIOSITY_GAP.label: Curiosity Gap
CURIOSITY_GAP.description: Creates an unresolved information tension the brain cannot ignore. The viewer is physiologically unable to scroll past without resolving it.
CURIOSITY_GAP.psychology: Loewenstein's information gap theory: the gap between what we know and want to know creates irresolvable tension. Resolution requires engagement. Brains are wired to seek closure.
CURIOSITY_GAP.activation_condition: Something surprising, counterintuitive, or visually incomplete must appear. The resolution must be WITHHELD — hinted at but not given.
CURIOSITY_GAP.image_rule: Show activity at 30-60% completion. The shape is emerging, the path half-traced. Visual incompleteness IS the trigger — the brain mentally completes it and gets the dopamine.
caption_opener_examples: He figured out the answer before I did. I'm still thinking about why.
This maze stumped 3 out of 4 adults. Kids under 7 solved it in under 2 minutes.
There's one hidden word in here nobody finds on the first try.

CHALLENGE.label: Challenge Activation
CHALLENGE.description: Activates competitive ego simultaneously in parent ('my kid is smart') and child ('I want to beat this'). Comments become scorecards. Sharing = social currency.
CHALLENGE.psychology: Achievement motivation + social comparison. Challenge language activates effort investment. Comment bait is organic — 'Mine got it in 45 seconds!' Everyone wants to show up.
CHALLENGE.activation_condition: Must present a specific challenge with a difficulty signal. Success must feel possible but not guaranteed. The child must be SEEN trying — not having already won.
CHALLENGE.image_rule: Child at PEAK ENGAGEMENT — brow furrowed, pencil moving, body leaning in. NO finished puzzles. The challenge must be LIVE in the frame.
caption_opener_examples: Can your kid solve this in under 2 minutes?
1 in 5 kids gets this right on the first try. Is yours one of them?
This took our 5-year-old 3 minutes. It took me 6. Drop yours below.

NOSTALGIA.label: Nostalgia Reframe
NOSTALGIA.description: Connects a childhood memory to the present moment. Nostalgia is the highest-intensity emotional trigger — limbic system activation. Shared as a gift: 'you need to feel this too.'
NOSTALGIA.psychology: Nostalgia is self-relevant and bittersweet — it increases social connectedness and meaning-making. Shared nostalgia becomes communal identity. Viral because it feels like a gift, not content.
NOSTALGIA.activation_condition: Must reference a specific, sensory memory parents of 4-8 year olds (ages 25-40) would recognize. Saturday mornings. A box of crayons. Grandmother's house. The smell of paper.
NOSTALGIA.image_rule: Warm, golden, slightly imperfect lighting — NOT Instagram-polished. The activity (crayons, puzzle, pencil) should look analog, worn, timeless. Like something that has always existed.
caption_opener_examples: Somewhere in your memory, there's a quiet Saturday morning. This felt like that.
Remember when a box of crayons felt like the whole world?
My grandmother had a drawer full of activity books. Hours went soft in that drawer.

DEV_FOMO.label: Developmental FOMO
DEV_FOMO.description: Fear of missing a developmental window. Content gets saved because it has DEFERRED VALUE — 'I need to do this with my kid. Not now, but this weekend.' That save IS the conversion.
DEV_FOMO.psychology: Loss aversion applied to parenting. Parents feel responsible for developmental windows. 'Meaty enough to need again later' = Pinterest's #1 save trigger. Specific skill names activate the FOMO reflex.
DEV_FOMO.activation_condition: Must name a specific developmental skill (fine motor, spatial reasoning, executive function, phonemic awareness) with a genuine, non-fabricated connection to the activity.
DEV_FOMO.image_rule: The ACTIVITY is the visual hero — well-lit, beautiful, clearly visible. It should look like something worth printing and using TODAY. Child engaged but secondary. The printable IS the product.
caption_opener_examples: The 10-minute activity that builds the skill reading specialists actually recommend.
Fine motor development doesn't need a class. It needs a pencil and a good puzzle.
They're not just coloring. They're building the hand control that makes writing easier.

COMPLETION_SATISFACTION.label: Completion Satisfaction
COMPLETION_SATISFACTION.description: The visceral dopamine of watching something become complete. Mirror neurons fire. No cognitive effort required. Addictive by design — the reward comes from the act of witnessing completion.
COMPLETION_SATISFACTION.psychology: Zeigarnik reversed: completing the incomplete triggers disproportionate reward. ASMR research: satisfying completion videos achieve 78% average watch time. The brain NEEDS to see it finish.
COMPLETION_SATISFACTION.activation_condition: Must show OR strongly imply a satisfying completion arc. Progression (blank → finished) OR near-completion where the viewer mentally completes it.
COMPLETION_SATISFACTION.image_rule: For static images: 80-90% complete state — the finish line visible, the viewer's brain crosses it automatically. For video: the reveal IS the content. Never show blank, never show mid-frustration.
caption_opener_examples: Blank page. Pencil in hand. Twenty minutes later — something that was nothing.
Watch what happens when they connect the last dot.
She held it up. 'I made this,' she said. She had.
